note_en:
Twitter  (TWTR.N) on Tuesday introduced an "Arabic (feminine)" language setting enabling the social media site to speak to users using feminine grammar, part of what it said was an inclusion and diversity drive.
 "We want our service to reflect the voices that shape the conversations that take place on our service," said Rasha Fawakhiri, Twitter's communications head for the Middle East and North Africa.
 In Arabic, verbs agree with the gender of their subject. Masculine forms are used to address mixed or unknown audiences and are the default in most texts.
 Twitter did not introduce a non-binary gender language option in Arabic, but Fawakhiri said the company has other gender neutral projects in the works for the site. It has plans to add a designated gender pronoun field to Twitter profiles so people can display how they prefer to be addressed.
 Until now, the instruction for the user to Tweet in Arabic had appeared only in the masculine form "gharrid". With a change of settings, this command can now appear on Twitter as "gharridi", the feminine form.
 Twitter says it is the first social media site to introduce an "Arabic (feminine)" language option. Dubai-based global logistics company Aramex  (ARMX.DU) in April added a similar language option to its corporate website.
 "We want to provide people with the option of how they prefer to be addressed," Fawakhiri said.
 Twitter started with Arabic and the initiative could be expanded to other languages, Fawakhiri said.
 Last year the company amended some of the language used by its engineers in their processes to be more inclusive. This included "they/them/their" pronouns and replacing "man hours" with "person or engineer hours", and "master/slave" with "leader/follower".
title: Twitter在多样性驱动器中添加了“阿拉伯语（女性）”语言选项
title_en: Twitter adds 'Arabic (feminine)' language option in diversity drive

当年春天关闭纽约时，许多居民依靠他们从未听说过的巨大建筑：JFK8，亚马逊在美国最大的城市唯一的履行中心。
 内部发生了什么显示杰夫贝斯如何创造了未来的工作场所，并在大流行期间撤下了不可能的事情 - 而且还揭示了他员工所做更好的方式的方式。
 每年，数十万名工人通过雇用和监测，纪律和火灾的巨大机制流失。在大流行中，已经紧张的系统蹒跚而行。
   去年9月，安卡斯蒂略从亚马逊看到一封电子邮件，没有任何意义。她的丈夫为公司工作了五年，最近在STENEN岛上的超级化仓库，作为零售商的纽约市的关键管道。现在它希望他回到夜班。
 “我们通知了您的经理和H.R.关于您在10月1日上班的回程工作，”消息表示。
 卡斯蒂略女士非常令人难以置信。在春季工作的强制性加班时，她42岁的丈夫Alberto一直是该网站上第一波员工，以测试冠状病毒的阳性。他遭受了繁荣和感染，他遭受了广泛的脑损伤。关于响应能力的测试，Castillo女士说：“他的分数几乎没有。”
  几个月来，卡斯蒂略女士，礼貌，一点的物理治疗师一直在提醒公司，她的丈夫一直为零售巨人为零售业而自豪地进行自豪。响应令人沮丧和令人困惑。电子邮件和浏览亚马逊的自动化系统经常死亡。公司的福利是慷慨的，但她一直陷入恐慌，因为残疾支付神秘地停止了。她设法与几位人力资源工人交谈，其中一个人恢复了付款，但之后，对话大部分地恢复到她丈夫的手机上的电话树，自动回复和语音邮件，询问他是否回到了。 
回归工作传票加深了她怀疑，即亚马逊没有完全注册他的情况。 “他们没有跟踪发生在他身上的事情吗？”她说。她想问一下公司：“你的工人是一次性吗？你能替换它们吗？“
 Castillo先生的工作场所是美国最大的城市唯一的亚马逊履行中心，在大流行期间取得了不可能。随着纽约的经典产业遭受大规模崩溃，仓库，叫做JFK8，吸收的酒店工作人员，演员，调酒师和舞者，每小时支付近18美元。通过新的使命感推动客户害怕亲自购物，JFK8帮助了亚马逊粉碎运输记录，达到了平流层销售和预订，相当于前三年的利润滚入一个。
 成功，速度和敏捷性是可能的，因为亚马逊及其创始人杰夫贝斯通过技术开创了新的批量管理人员的新方法，依赖于最小化人类接触的系统迷宫，以便不受约束。
 然而，根据纽约时报在去年的纽约时报审查JFK8的纽约时报的纽约时报，该公司在局外人看不到的方式摇摇欲坠。
 与其精确的，复杂的包装加工相比，亚马逊管理人员的模型 - 非常依赖度量，应用程序和聊天行程 - 甚至在冠心病抵达之前甚至不均匀，并且员工经常必须充当自己的案例工作者，采访和记录表演。在大流行中，亚马逊的系统通过工人燃烧，导致无意中的射击和停滞不前的福利，并阻碍了沟通，对年龄的商业成功故事施放了阴影。
 亚马逊在公司前所未有的步骤提供宽大，但随时有时矛盾或结束它们。卡斯蒂略先生在JFK8的工作人员被告知要尽可能多地休息，然后强制加班。当亚马逊提供员工灵活的个人叶子时，系统处理它们的系统，向工人发出暴雪，向工人发出暴风雪通知，并将员工争抢宣传，以保存它们，根据人力资源和仓库员工。
  在缺席最初飙升和扰乱运输后，亚马逊留下了员工，主要是在黑暗中关于病毒的收费。该公司没有告诉JFK8或其他仓库的工人案件的数量，导致他们担心关于“个人”的通知是关于“个人”的态度意味着两年或22日。亚马逊公开表示，它透露了纽约卫生官员的确认案件，而纽约市记录暂无报告的案件直到11月。公司和城市官员争论发生的事情。 
亚马逊继续追踪大多数仓库工人的每一分钟，从他们暂停的速度快速，他们暂停了多久 - 这种监测被激发失败的联盟驱动器，在这个春天的阿拉巴马州仓库中受到了沮丧的黑人员工。如果生产率标记，亚马逊的计算机认为工人应该责备。在大流行早期，在线零售商暂停了员工的低产量，但这种变化在JFK8没有明确宣布，因此一些工人仍然担心移动太慢的慢慢将其生计。
 “这是一个非常重要的是，地区经理了解联营公司的数字不仅仅是数字，”一名员工在秋天的秋季汇率上写道，补充说：“我们是人类。我们不是用来制作日常/每周目标和率的工具。“
 公司吹捧令人叹为观止的工作创作号码：从7月到10月2020年，它畅销35万名新工人，超过圣路易斯人口。许多新兵 - 聘请通过计算机筛选，很少的谈话或审查 - 持续几天或几周。
 甚至在大流行，以前未报告的数据显示之前，亚马逊每周丢失约3％的小时员工，这意味着其劳动力之间的营业额大约是每年150％。该速度，零售和物流行业的速度几乎是两倍，使一些高管担心跨越美国的工人。
 在记录大流行风险如何暴露亚马逊的就业制度的权力和危险的情况下，记者采访了近200名现任和前雇员，从JFK8巴士站到了海外的后勤工作人员到了Staten Island和西雅图的经理。次时报还审查了公司文件，法律申请和政府记录，以及仓库反馈委员会的帖子，作为工人关注的实时股票。
  今年4月，贝佐斯先生说，他为公司的工作文化感到自豪，“可实现的”生产力目标，薪酬和福利。在采访中，仓库和JFK8总经理人力资源负责人表示，该公司优先考虑员工福利，并指出它已经扩大了其H.R.员工，并引用了展示高级工作人员的内部调查。来自JFK8的一些管理人员及以外地描述了与他们的团队建立了深厚的关系。
 亚马逊承认有些问题，无意中的火灾，丧失福利，工作放弃通知和叶子，但拒绝透露有多少人受到影响。发言人Kelly Nantel建议那些问题和其他一些在本文中复杂的问题是异常值。 
H.R.领导者的Ofori Agboka指出，社会疏散和掩蔽使得在大流行期间以个人方式聘用员工。他说，“98％的一切都是伟大的 - 人们正在拥有正确的经历，”在他们想要的时候得到他们需要的帮助。
 但是有几位前高管帮助设计亚马逊的系统，仍然称自己是公司的崇拜者，表示高营业额，压力过度的生产力和扩大的后果对忽视的影响太为严重。本公司尚未展示这些问题，说保罗·特罗，曾最近领导致力于了解仓库工人的公司团队。
 “亚马逊可以解决它的任何问题，它在接受采访时说。但是，人力资源部门靠近亚马逊的重点，严谨和投资，他以前工作过。 “它觉得我在一个不同的公司中，”他说。
 亚马逊建立仓库资源运营的前亚马逊副总裁David Niekerk表示，一些问题源于公司在较小的时候开发的想法。贝佐斯先生并不希望被根深蒂固的劳动力，称为“三月到平庸”，尼克克先生召回，并看到低技能的工作，相对短期。正如亚马逊迅速增长的那样，Niekerk先生说，其政策更加努力地实施公平和护理。 “这只是一个数字游戏，”他说。 “文化迷失了。”
  即使是Bezos先生，他的最后一圈是他所创造的公司首席执行官，现在正在令他令人意外的让他发明的系统的让步。在最近给股东的一封信中，他表示，联盟的努力表明，“我们需要更好的愿景，了解我们如何为员工创造价值 - 这是他们成功的愿景。”
 “我们一直想成为地球最客户的公司，”他写道。现在，他补充说：“我们将成为地球最好的雇主和地球最安全的工作地点。”
 亚马逊也在步伐上成为全国最大的私人雇主，因为它继续扩大。大约一百万人在美国，他们中的大多数小时工人，现在依靠公司的工资和福利。许多人描述了奖励的工作。 adama ndoye在远程学院的同时支持她的家人，她的JFK8支付。 “灯，食物，衣服，一切，”她说。一位厨师黎明乔治表示，她感谢JFK8在酒店厨房工作消失后迎接她的赛后。 “我愿意为每小时的收入脱掉袜子，”她说。 
有些人钦佩亚马逊的野心。 “这就像是每晚有一场比赛的球队中的投手，”丹卡克纳罗说，他于2018年开业并与卡斯蒂略先生合作。
  但是，卡格纳罗先生在7月份被错误地解雇，同时试图从休假回来，无法与任何人一起帮助。
 “请注意以下内容，”他在最终的未解决电子邮件请求中写道。 “我希望留在亚马逊。”
   在2020年3月下旬，Traci Weishalla走了JFK8的长度，将标志着她作为经理标志着她的荧光背心。她想要一个未经过滤的看看她很快就会帮助监督：一个仓库的大小为15个足球场，服务于美国最大的大都市，就像它成为大流行的国家震中。
 来自围绕包裹鞭打的传送带的噪音就像迎面而来的地铁列车的咆哮。建造以征服该国最有利可图的市场，设施每周七天凌晨24小时跑。
 魏斯巴拉女士帮助开设了一年半的仓库，现在 - 作为全国的家庭客户，克制了温度计，消毒剂和谜题 - 她在她的回归作为助理总经理中看到了机会和目的。她说，对于处理物流奇迹的组织，冠状病毒只是克服的另一个障碍。
 “这就是我们所做的，”威士那女士，38岁以后解释。 “我们努力弄清楚不可能的问题。” 
但亚马逊的强大系统很懒散。半卡车坐在全国各地的仓库，没有足够的工人卸下它们。客户发现，公司被视为非无需的物品可能需要一个月到达 - 这是一个在两天内经常交付的企业的永恒。
   为了引诱他们，亚马逊提供临时2美元的时间提高，加班费双重支付，并首次无限制地休息。高管认为工人们应该能够在没有害怕被解雇的情况下留在家，并且具有更大的灵活性，据熟悉该决定的两个人表示，有些人可能仍然可以参加部分转变。 （就像本文中的其他一些高级领导人一样，他们就匿名的条件谈到了，因为他们没有被授权发表评论。）
  在全国各地，亚马逊50,000名工人的近三分之一都在家。据前招聘人员表示，一些新的雇用在他们甚至开始之前被遗弃的工作。 JFK8“就像一个鬼城”，留下了一名留下的工人亚瑟特·特纳。
 甚至阿尔贝托卡斯蒂略也被认为是住宅。新闻的数字是不可思议的：至少有20,000名已经感染的纽约人，城市医院卡住了，全国预测了170万人死亡。
 但这是没有收入的没有时间：卡斯蒂利洛斯，来自菲律宾的移民，渴望买房子。同事们说，他在夜间，故障排除和训练，经常掌握，经常笑话和“星球大战”的参考文献，他刚刚申请了促销活动。
 JFK8也在给予矛盾的指示：尽管亚马逊承诺未付休假的承诺，但工人得到了认知每个部门的强制性加班。
 当卡斯蒂略先生于3月24日抵达时，他听到仓库有第一个积极案例。他让他的老板搞砸了，谁回答说，“是的，忘了带上这一点，”并补充说，在雇员合作的每个人都得到了通知。卡斯蒂略先生称他的妻子讨论是否回家。他们决定完成他的转变。 
那天早上，两名工人沿着相反的方向开车，杰出的休息室，并告诉了几十个同事：病毒已经违反了仓库，亚马逊不能值得信任，告诉他们真理，设施应该被关闭。
 德里克·帕尔默和克里斯·小型，亚马逊队友和最好的朋友，并不是任何正式努力的一部分。他们的雇主认为工会是一个可怕的威胁，甚至在潜在的劳动组织计划方面支持在纽约的第二次总部。零售工人联盟曾经大胆地宣布，JFK8将成为该国的第一联盟亚马逊仓库，但努力已经死亡。
 自2015年以来，两名男子都在亚马逊，并从最低梯级上了解公司。帕尔默先生，那么31，令人患观察者和故意，如此适合他经常在10小时轮班后去健身房。在放弃社区学院后，他在一串仓库工作，加入了亚马逊，现在是JFK8的“选择器”，将产品从机器人架上拉动。他经常在跟踪生产力的软件上生产顶部数字，并被选择培训其他人并帮助伊利诺伊州的仓库。
 他也觉得让人失望，相信亚马逊的耸立成功并没有给像他这样的工人累积。他说，员工在很大程度上受到应用程序，算法和严格但解释的规则很差。他说，当他在2019年举行2019年举行的卫生部遇到威士拉女士时，他说，他说，他要求更多人类互动管理，并告诉她，他渴望像她一样的工作。但他没有看到没有变化。 “如果我们超越要求，就没有奖励，”他在采访时说。
  当帕尔默先生上次寻求促销时，在2020年初，他是申请该职位的382人中。虽然他不知道它，但是巨大的设计是陡峭的，贝佐斯先生的管理理念。
 亚马逊故意有限于每小时工人的移动性，前者是杰克克先生，前任于2016年在本公司近17年后退休。随后戴夫克拉克经营主管，左右掀起了他的建议，左右占据了每小时员工的更多领导作用，类似于军队中的非议长，他回忆道。
 相反，克拉克先生是亚马逊的消费者业务的首席执行官，希望在聘请“邪恶的智慧”前线管理人员直接出于大学，纳基克说。相比之下，沃尔玛美国商店的超过75％的经理开始作为每小时员工。在亚马逊的模式之后，JFK8去年在其超过5000名员工中推动了220人，这是沃尔玛的一半的速度。 
亚马逊的亚马逊的创始人并不希望每小时努力坚持长期以来一直盯着威胁的“大，不满”劳动力，尼克克先生召回。他说，公司数据显示，随着时间的推移，大多数员工都变得不​​那么渴望，并且贝佐斯先生认为人们本身懒惰。 “他会说的是，我们的性质是人类的是尽可能地消耗尽可能多的能量来获得我们想要的东西。”从整个业务中嵌入了这一定罪，从即时命令到普遍使用数据来充分利用员工。
 所以保证工资增加三年后停止，亚马逊为低技能员工提供了激励措施。每年，Palmer先生看到标志上涨了数千美元的辞职，并且在他每天早上进入JFK8时，他通过了一个课堂，以便在其他领域训练他们。
 “埃比克先生，”埃尔··联系人“，虽然公司向亚马逊提供培训和职业对那些感兴趣的人表示，但它很自豪地为人们提供”季节和时间“的人需要的短期就业。
  由于病毒抵达JFK8，帕尔默先生担心亚马逊如何保护和沟通工人。关于仓库第一个积极案例的通知已经不均匀。一位同事在先生靠近的一家工作，似乎病了，她的眼睛血腥，因为她通过她的转变挣扎。
 这两个人只看到一个解决方案：对于JFK8暂停，清洁和重新评估，因为Queens的亚马逊设施已经短暂完成。无偿休假是不够的，他们说 - 一家由地球上最富有的人经营的公司不应该强迫工人选择安全和薪水。
    “你们真的只是挑选必备物品吗？”一个人问，指亚马逊的早期大流行努力只发货，只能发货。
  几乎所有人的所有工人都是黑色的，就像帕尔默先生和小学生或拉丁美洲先生一样。根据2019年的内部亚马逊记录，据亚马逊记录有超过60％的员工伙伴员。管理层，文件展示，超过70％的白色或亚洲人。记录显示，JFK8的黑人伙伴近50％近50％，更有可能被解雇 - 无论是生产力，不当行为，还是没有出现的工作，而不是他们的白色同行。 （亚马逊表示，在不了解其来源的更多细节，无法确认数据。）

 在不断的监测之间，许多工人是懒散的假设，以及缺乏进步机会，“很多少数民族工人才被使用，”帕尔默先生稍后说。
 “我们是那座建筑的心灵和灵魂，”他在聊天中写道。 “没有我们，没有什么可以完成。”
 这两个人继续他们的休息室警告几天，并面对JFK8经理。 “如果上帝禁止，这座建筑物中的某人越来越多，或者某人被爱的人远离，那将在你的手上，而不是我的，”这对阵的爆竹先生，令人谨慎的是，这对雷克斯的最高领导者告诉仓库的最高领导者一个对话的音频录制。
 3月30日，他们在停车场展示了一小群其他员工。帕尔默先生携带了一个读书的标志，“像你的客户一样对待你的工人。”
 在西雅图，高管仍在努力参加院票考虑，试图尽量减少抗议，而是更多地关注它。亚马逊发射了小学生，称他的示范基于与病人的同事联系违反了检疫秩序。 （帕尔默先生收到了侵犯社会D的警告

When the coronavirus shut down New York last spring, many residents came to rely on a colossal building they had never heard of: JFK8, Amazon’s only fulfillment center in America’s largest city.
 What happened inside shows how Jeff Bezos created the workplace of the future and pulled off the impossible during the pandemic — but also reveals what’s standing in the way of his promise to do better by his employees.
 Each year, hundreds of thousands of workers churn through a vast mechanism that hires and monitors, disciplines and fires. Amid the pandemic, the already strained system lurched.
   Last September, Ann Castillo saw an email from Amazon that made no sense. Her husband had worked for the company for five years, most recently at the supersize warehouse on Staten Island that served as the retailer’s critical pipeline to New York City. Now it wanted him back on the night shift.
 “We notified your manager and H.R. about your return to work on Oct. 1, 2020,” the message said.
 Ms. Castillo was incredulous. While working mandatory overtime in the spring, her 42-year-old husband, Alberto, had been among the first wave of employees at the site to test positive for the coronavirus. Ravaged by fevers and infections, he suffered extensive brain damage. On tests of responsiveness, Ms. Castillo said, “his score was almost nothing.”
  For months, Ms. Castillo, a polite, get-it-done physical therapist, had been alerting the company that her husband, who had been proud to work for the retail giant, was severely ill. The responses were disjointed and confusing. Emails and calls to Amazon’s automated systems often dead-ended. The company’s benefits were generous, but she had been left panicking as disability payments mysteriously halted. She managed to speak to several human resources workers, one of whom reinstated the payments, but after that, the dialogue mostly reverted to phone trees, auto-replies and voice mail messages on her husband’s phone asking if he was coming back.
 The return-to-work summons deepened her suspicion that Amazon didn’t fully register his situation. “Haven’t they kept track of what happened to him?” she said. She wanted to ask the company: “Are your workers disposable? Can you just replace them?”
 Mr. Castillo’s workplace, the only Amazon fulfillment center in America’s largest city, was achieving the impossible during the pandemic. With New York’s classic industries suffering mass collapse, the warehouse, called JFK8, absorbed hotel workers, actors, bartenders and dancers, paying nearly $18 an hour. Driven by a new sense of mission to serve customers afraid to shop in person, JFK8 helped Amazon smash shipping records, reach stratospheric sales and book the equivalent of the previous three years’ profits rolled into one.
 That success, speed and agility were possible because Amazon and its founder, Jeff Bezos, had pioneered new ways of mass-managing people through technology, relying on a maze of systems that minimized human contact to grow unconstrained.
 But the company was faltering in ways outsiders could not see, according to a New York Times examination of JFK8 over the last year.
 In contrast to its precise, sophisticated processing of packages, Amazon’s model for managing people — heavily reliant on metrics, apps and chatbots — was uneven and strained even before the coronavirus arrived, with employees often having to act as their own caseworkers, interviews and records show. Amid the pandemic, Amazon’s system burned through workers, resulted in inadvertent firings and stalled benefits, and impeded communication, casting a shadow over a business success story for the ages.
 Amazon took steps unprecedented at the company to offer leniency, but then at times contradicted or ended them. Workers like Mr. Castillo at JFK8 were told to take as much unpaid time off as they needed, then hit with mandatory overtime. When Amazon offered employees flexible personal leaves, the system handling them jammed, issuing a blizzard of job-abandonment notices to workers and sending staff scrambling to save them, according to human resources and warehouse employees.
  After absences initially soared and disrupted shipping, Amazon left employees mostly in the dark about the toll of the virus. The company did not tell workers at JFK8 or other warehouses the number of cases, causing them to worry whether notifications about “individuals” testing positive meant two or 22. While Amazon said publicly that it was disclosing confirmed cases to health officials, New York City records show no reported cases until November. The company and city officials dispute what happened.
 Amazon continued to track every minute of most warehouse workers’ shifts, from how fast they packed merchandise to how long they paused — the kind of monitoring that  spurred a failed unionization drive led by frustrated Black employees at an Alabama warehouse this spring. If productivity flagged, Amazon’s computers assumed the worker was to blame. Early in the pandemic, the online retailer paused its firing of employees for low output, but that change was not announced clearly at JFK8, so some workers still feared that moving too slowly would cost them their livelihoods.
 “It is very important that area managers understand that associates are more than just numbers,” an employee wrote on JFK8’s internal feedback board last fall, adding: “We are human beings. We are not tools used to make their daily/weekly goals and rates.”
 The company touted breathtaking job-creation numbers: From July to October 2020 alone, it scooped up 350,000 new workers, more than the population of St. Louis. Many recruits — hired through a computer screening, with little conversation or vetting — lasted just days or weeks.
 Even before the pandemic, previously unreported data shows, Amazon lost about 3 percent of its hourly associates each week, meaning the turnover among its work force was roughly 150 percent a year. That rate,  almost double that of the retail and logistics industries, has made some executives worry about running out of workers across America.
 In documenting the untold story of how the pandemic exposed the power and peril of Amazon’s employment system, reporters interviewed nearly 200 current and former employees, from new hires at the JFK8 bus stop to back-office workers overseas to managers on Staten Island and in Seattle. The Times also reviewed company documents, legal filings and government records, as well as posts from warehouse feedback boards that served as a real-time ticker of worker concerns.
  This April, Mr. Bezos said he was proud of the company’s work culture, the “achievable” productivity goals, the pay and benefits. In interviews, the head of human resources for warehouses and the general manager of JFK8 said that the company prioritized employee welfare, noted that it had expanded its H.R. staff and cited internal surveys showing high worker satisfaction. Some managers from JFK8 and beyond described building deep relationships with their teams.
 Amazon acknowledged some issues with inadvertent firings, loss of benefits, job abandonment notices and leaves, but declined to disclose how many people were affected. Kelly Nantel, a spokeswoman, suggested that those problems and some others chronicled in this article were outliers.
 Ofori Agboka, the H.R. leader, noted that social distancing and masking had made it harder to engage employees in personal ways during the pandemic. Still, he said, “98 percent of everything’s going great — people are having the right experiences,” getting the help they need when they want it.
 But several former executives who helped design Amazon’s systems, and still call themselves admirers of the company, said the high turnover, pressure over productivity and consequences of scaling up have become too critical to ignore. The company has not ambitiously addressed those issues, said Paul Stroup, who until recently led corporate teams devoted to understanding warehouse workers.
 “Amazon can solve pretty much any problem it puts its mind behind,” he said in an interview. The human resources division, though, had nowhere near the focus, rigor and investment of Amazon’s logistical operations, where he had previously worked. “It felt like I was in a different company,” he said.
 David Niekerk, a former Amazon vice president who built the warehouse human resources operations, said that some problems stemmed from ideas the company had developed when it was much smaller. Mr. Bezos did not want an entrenched work force, calling it “a march to mediocrity,” Mr. Niekerk recalled, and saw low-skilled jobs as relatively short-term. As Amazon rapidly grew, Mr. Niekerk said, its policies were harder to implement with fairness and care. “It is just a numbers game in many ways,” he said. “The culture gets lost.”
  Even Mr. Bezos, in his final lap as chief executive of the company he created, is now making startling concessions about the system he invented. In a recent letter to shareholders, he said the union effort showed that “we need a better vision for how we create value for employees — a vision for their success.”
 “We have always wanted to be Earth’s most customer-centric company,” he wrote. Now, he added, “we are going to be Earth’s best employer and Earth’s  safest place to work.”
 Amazon is also  on pace to become the nation’s largest private employer within a year or two, as it continues expanding. About a million people in the United States, most of them hourly workers, now rely on the company’s wages and benefits. Many describe the job as rewarding. Adama Ndoye had supported her family on her JFK8 pay while attending college remotely. “Lights on, food, clothes, everything,” she said. Dawn George, a chef, said she was grateful to JFK8 for taking her in after hotel kitchen jobs disappeared last spring. “I’m willing to work my socks off just for an hourly income,” she said.
 Some admire Amazon’s ambition. “It was like being a pitcher on a team that had a game every night,” said Dan Cavagnaro, who started at JFK8 when it opened in 2018 and worked with Mr. Castillo.
  But Mr. Cavagnaro was mistakenly fired in July while trying to return from leave, and could not reach anyone to help.
 “Please note the following,” he wrote in his final, unanswered email plea. “I WISH TO REMAIN EMPLOYED WITH AMAZON.”
   In late March 2020, Traci Weishalla walked the length of JFK8, forgoing the fluorescent vest that marked her as a manager. She wanted an unfiltered look at what she would soon be helping to oversee: a warehouse the size of 15 football fields, serving America’s largest metropolis just as it was becoming the national epicenter of the pandemic.
 The noise, from conveyor belts whipping around packages, was like the roar of an oncoming subway train. Built to conquer the most lucrative market in the country, the facility ran almost 24 hours a day, seven days a week.
 Ms. Weishalla had helped open the warehouse a year and a half earlier, and now — as homebound customers across the nation  clamored for thermometers, disinfectant and puzzles — she saw opportunity and purpose in her return as assistant general manager. For an organization that dealt in logistical miracles, the coronavirus was just another obstacle to overcome, she said.
 “That’s what we do,” Ms. Weishalla, 38, explained later. “We work to figure out the impossible problems.”
  But Amazon’s mighty system was lurching. Semi trucks sat at warehouses around the country, without enough workers to unload them. Customers discovered that items the company had deemed nonessential might take a month to arrive — an eternity for a business that had routinely delivered within two days.
   To lure them back, Amazon offered a temporary $2-an-hour raise, double pay for overtime and, for the first time, unlimited unpaid time off. Executives thought that workers should be able to stay home without fear of being fired, and that with greater flexibility, some might still come in for part of a shift, according to two people familiar with the decision. (Like some other senior leaders in this article, they spoke on the condition of anonymity because they were not authorized to comment.)
  Across the country, almost a third of Amazon’s 500,000 workers were staying home. Some new hires abandoned jobs before they even began, according to former recruiters. JFK8 “was like a ghost town,” recalled Arthur Turner, a worker who remained.
 Even Alberto Castillo considered staying home. The numbers on the news were unfathomable: at least 20,000 New Yorkers already infected, city hospitals jammed, as many as 1.7 million deaths projected nationwide.
 But this was no time to go without his income: The Castillos, immigrants from the Philippines, yearned to buy a house. He worked nights, troubleshooting and training with gentle mastery, frequent jokes and “Star Wars” references, colleagues said, and he had just applied for a promotion.
 JFK8 was also giving contradictory instructions: Despite Amazon’s promise of unpaid time off, workers were alerted that every department would be on mandatory overtime.
 When Mr. Castillo arrived on March 24, he heard the warehouse had its first positive case. He messaged his boss, who replied, “Yes, forgot to bring that up,” and added that everyone who worked with the employee had been notified. Mr. Castillo called his wife to discuss whether to head home. They decided he would finish out his shift.
    That morning, two workers drove in the opposite direction, beelined to JFK8’s break room and told dozens of colleagues: The virus had breached the warehouse, Amazon could not be trusted to tell them the truth and the facility should be shut down.
 Derrick Palmer and Chris Smalls, Amazon teammates and best friends, weren’t part of any formal effort. Their employer considered unionization a dire threat, and had even backed out of building a second headquarters in New York  in part over potential labor-organizing plans. A  retail workers’ union had once boldly declared that JFK8 would become the first unionized Amazon warehouse in the country, but the effort had died.
 Both men had been at Amazon since 2015 and knew the company from the lowest rungs. Mr. Palmer, then 31, was observant and deliberate, so fit that he often headed to the gym after a 10-hour shift. After dropping out of community college, he worked in a string of warehouses, joined Amazon and was now a “picker” at JFK8, pulling products off robotic shelves. He often produced top numbers on the software that tracked productivity, and had been selected to train others and help open a warehouse in Illinois.
 He also felt let down, believing that Amazon’s towering success didn’t accrue to workers like him. Employees felt managed largely by app, algorithm and strict but poorly explained rules, he said. When he met Ms. Weishalla at a 2019 session for workers to share feedback, he said, he requested more human interaction from management and told her he aspired to a job like hers. But he saw no changes. “If we go beyond the requirements, there’s no reward,” he said in an interview.
  When Mr. Palmer last sought a promotion, in early 2020, he was among 382 people who applied for the position. Though he didn’t know it, the odds were steep by design, an outgrowth of Mr. Bezos’ management philosophies.
 Amazon intentionally limited upward mobility for hourly workers, said Mr. Niekerk, the former H.R. vice president who retired in 2016 after nearly 17 years at the company. Dave Clark, then head of operations, had shot down his proposal around 2014 to create more leadership roles for hourly employees, similar to noncommissioned officers in the military, he recalled.
 Instead, Mr. Clark, who is now chief executive of Amazon’s consumer business, wanted to double down on hiring “wicked smart” frontline managers straight out of college, Mr. Niekerk said. By contrast, more than 75 percent of managers in Walmart’s U.S. stores started as hourly employees. Following a pattern across Amazon, JFK8 promoted 220 people last year among its more than 5,000 employees, a rate that is less than half of Walmart’s.
 Amazon’s founder didn’t want hourly workers to stick around for long, viewing “a large, disgruntled” work force as a threat, Mr. Niekerk recalled. Company data showed that most employees became less eager over time, he said, and Mr. Bezos believed that people were inherently lazy. “What he would say is that our nature as humans is to expend as little energy as possible to get what we want or need.” That conviction was  embedded throughout the business, from the ease of instant ordering to the pervasive use of data to get the most out of employees.
 So guaranteed wage increases stopped after three years, and Amazon provided incentives for low-skilled employees to leave. Every year, Mr. Palmer saw signs go up offering associates thousands of dollars to resign, and as he entered JFK8 each morning, he passed a classroom for free courses to train them in other fields.
 Mr. Agboka, the H.R. leader, said while the company offered training and careers at Amazon to those interested, it was proud to also provide people short-term employment for the “seasons and periods of time” they need.
  As the virus arrived at JFK8, Mr. Palmer worried about how Amazon would protect and communicate with workers. Notification about the warehouse’s first positive case had been uneven. A colleague working near Mr. Smalls had appeared sick, her eyes bloodshot as she struggled through her shift.
 The two men saw only one solution: for JFK8 to pause, clean and reassess, as an Amazon facility in Queens had briefly done. Unpaid leave wasn’t enough, they said — a company run by the richest man on earth shouldn’t force workers to choose between safety and a paycheck.
    “Are you guys actually just picking essential items?” one asked, referring to Amazon’s early-pandemic efforts to ship only necessary merchandise.
  Nearly all the workers in the group were Black, like Mr. Palmer and Mr. Smalls, or Latino. So were more than 60 percent of associates at JFK8, according to internal Amazon records from 2019. Management, the documents show, was more than 70 percent white or Asian. Black associates at JFK8 were almost 50 percent more likely to be fired — whether for productivity, misconduct, or not showing up for work — than their white peers, the records show. (Amazon said it could not confirm the data without knowing more specifics about its source.)
 Source: Amazon's 2018 report to the U.S. Equal Employment Opportunity Commission · Note: Bar heights are scaled by the total number of employees in each category. “Others” includes Native Hawaiians, Native Americans, Alaskan Natives and employees belonging to two or more groups.
 Between the constant monitoring, the assumption that many workers are slackers, and the lack of advancement opportunity, “a lot of minority workers just felt like we were being used,” Mr. Palmer said later.
 “We’re the heart and soul of that building,” he wrote in the chat. “Nothing gets done without us.”
 The two men continued their break-room warnings for several more days, and confronted JFK8 managers. “If, God forbid, somebody in this building passes away, or somebody’s loved one passes away, that’s going to be on your hands, not mine,” Mr. Smalls, the firecracker of the pair, told the warehouse’s top leader, according to an audio recording of one conversation.
 On March 30, they demonstrated in the parking lot with a small group of other employees. Mr. Palmer carried a sign that read, “Treat your workers like your customers.”
 In Seattle, executives still grappling with cratering attendance sought to minimize the protest but instead drew more  attention to it. Amazon fired Mr. Smalls, saying his demonstration had violated a quarantine order based on his contact with the sick co-worker. (Mr. Palmer received a warning for violating social-d

title_en: The Amazon That Customers Don't See

note: 他计算出奇怪的夸克颗粒会用戏剧性效果破坏地球：一吨规格将释放50千吨核弹的能量，沿着整个地球传播。 1993年，Vidgor Teplitz，Eugene Herrin，David Anderson和Ileana Tibuleac在美国南部卫理公会大学，开始寻找这样的事件。 他们搜查了世界和＃39;所谓的＆＃34的地震记录;未关联的事件＆＃34;他们查看了1990年至1993年至1993年之间的美国地质调查收集了超过一百万条记录，与传统的地震障碍，如地震无关。 以前，Herrin和Teplitz推测，可以寻找可能表明通过地球奇怪夸克物质（也称为弯曲）的地震事件，因为这些事件具有不同的地震信号 - 直线。 这种地震签名将是由核动物速度的大比率引起地球声速的速度。据估计，奇怪的夸克物质可能会在每秒400公里（每秒250英里），震动速度的40倍。 数据收集已停止该团队还确定了检测核的最低要求将通过七个监测站检测其信号。 研究人员最新调查结果单次出现两种地震事件，他们正在寻找线性图案。 在两种情况下，九个远吊站的到达时间和地震波的形式指向线性爆发的能量。随着典型地震的情况下，破裂撕开了数百公里的地球，而不是在表面附近压裂。 一项活动发生在1993年10月22日，据研究人员介绍，某些东西进入了南极洲的地球，并将其留在印度以后的0.73次以后。 另一个发生于1993年11月24日，当一个物体进入澳大利亚南部并在南极洲靠近南极洲的地球0.15后来。 第一个活动是在印度，澳大利亚，玻利维亚和土耳其的七个监测站记录，第二次活动被录制在澳大利亚和玻利维亚的九个监测站。 ＆＃34;我们可以证明这是奇怪的夸克问题，但这是迄今为止提供的唯一解释，＆＃34;赫兰辛说。 不幸的是，科学家可能无法找到更多的活动，建议通过地球通过奇怪的夸克。
note_en: He calculated that strange quark particles would dash through Earth with dramatic effect: a one-tonne spec would release the energy of a 50-kilotonne nuclear bomb, spread along its entire path through the Earth. In 1993, Vidgor Teplitz, Eugene Herrin, David Anderson and Ileana Tibuleac, all of the Southern Methodist University in the US, began looking for such events. They searched the world's seismographic records for so-called "unassociated events". They looked at more than a million records collected by the US Geological Survey between 1990 to 1993 that were not associated with traditional seismic disturbances, such as earthquakes. Previously, Herrin and Teplitz speculated that it would be possible to search for seismic events that might indicate passage of strange quark matter (also known as nuclearites) through the Earth because such events would have a distinct seismic signal - a straight line. This seismic signature would be caused by the large ratio of the nuclearites speed to the speed of sound in the Earth. It was estimated that the strange quark matter might pass through the earth at 400 km per second (250 miles per second), 40 times the speed of seismic waves. Data collection halted  The team also determined that the minimum requirement for detection of a nuclearite would be detection of its signal by seven monitoring stations. The researchers latest findings single out two seismic events with the linear pattern they were looking for.  In two cases, the arrival times and forms of seismic waves at nine far-flung stations pointed to linear bursts of energy. The ruptures ripped through the planet at hundreds of kilometres per second rather than fracturing only near the surface, as typical earthquakes do. One event occurred on 22 October 1993, when, according to the researchers, something entered the Earth off Antarctica and left it south of India 0.73 of a second later. The other occurred on 24 November 1993, when an object entered south of Australia and exited the Earth near Antarctica 0.15 of a second later. The first event was recorded at seven monitoring stations in India, Australia, Bolivia and Turkey, and the second event was recorded at nine monitoring stations in Australia and Bolivia. "We can't prove that this was strange quark matter, but that is the only explanation that has been offered so far," Herrin says. Unfortunately, scientists may not be able to find any more events that suggest the passage of strange quark matter through the Earth.
title: 夸克队经过地球吗？ （2002）
title_en: Did Quark Matter Pass Through the Earth? (2002)

note_en:
Let’s start with the software update. Last year, the company unveiled  Board, a visual interface that lets you work together during a video call. It lets you share ideas and collaborate using a whiteboard interface. You can create sticky notes, add text, insert images, move things around and start a video call from there.
 Other people on the calls are represented through tiny thumbnails so that you can remain focused on the digital whiteboard. You can also connect Board with your existing video-conferencing tool.
  This week, the company is updating Board and renaming it to Board Hybrid. “It’s the new version of Board that isn’t only designed for remote work, but also for hybrid work,” founder and CEO Matthieu Beucher said in a press conference.
 Board Hybrid users can now add any type of file to their whiteboard. This way, they don’t have to upload files to a shared drive, create a link and paste the link in the whiteboard. Users can preview PowerPoint presentations, Word files, spreadsheets and more directly from Klaxoon’s interface.
 There are some new drawing tools including some new connectors. For instance, you can create mindmaps from there. You can now also share your screen from Klaxoon’s own video-conferencing solution.
  The new product is something quite different — it’s a meeting room called Hybridity. It looks like a hexagon-shaped space capsule. There’s no window and it feels like a black box from the outside.
 Inside, you’ll find three seats, three screens, three cameras and three  Klaxoon Box devices. “Everyone can see everyone perfectly well and everybody can immerse themselves in content,” Beucher said.
 If you’ve joined a hybrid meeting from your home, you’re well aware of the issues involved with that setup. Part of the team is sitting in the same room. They look like tiny action figures and you can’t figure out who’s talking.
 With this setup, Klaxoon hopes it’ll be easier to run meetings with people in the office and people at home. A Klaxoon Hybridity conference room requires 5 square meters. You can put it down in a corner and move it to another location a couple of years later. It’s not secured in the ground.
 Pre-orders will start this week. The company expects to sell Hybridity with a subscription model with prices starting at €2,000 per month. It’s going to be interesting to see whether Klaxoon has found a new revenue stream of it it’s just a fun experiment. But it could replace those tiny phone booths in your office.
title: Klaxoon推出了混合工作的白板聚焦会议室
title_en: Klaxoon introduces whiteboard-focused conference room for hybrid work

  公司的资金和转换焦点强调了我们在去年消费者所看到的一些变化。
 在很大程度上，由于Covid-19，我们大多数人一直在留下回家 - 这常常也包括在搬到新房，以适应他们在他们身上的更多时间 - 而且随着ThumbTack看到它，这已经领导了更多的人要更加关注如何让那些家园更好的地方，并保持整体形状更好。这部分原因是为什么它看到一个大机会 - 作为一个市场来寻找专业人士的一个市场，你可能需要在房子周围完成 - 为提供“家庭管理”平台来满足新的关注。
 “这是狂野的，人们的家园是他们最大的资产，你对你负责的最复杂的事情，但它并没有带手动，”Zappacosta在接受采访时说。 “当你想到你的车时，你需要定期使用。否则，您可能必须支付大型紧急情况[修复某些问题]。同样的概念适用于家庭。但是从来没有一种简单的方法可以知道你应该做的事情，多少钱，然后轻松完成它。所以这就是我们正在做的事情。“
 他说，这些服务包括在家中的电气电气急诊修复，以及您可能需要对您的财产进行预防性维护。他们不包括承包商来改装浴室或其他选修工作。
 搬入家庭管理是一个有趣的转向另一个原因。这是一个符号，ThumbTack如何参加更大的变化，这些消费者在整体上与服务有多次参与服务：通过订阅。 
也就是说，虽然我们中的许多人仍然会出去购买物品，或者现在每天去看电影，我们也都订阅了至少一个视频点播服务，我们许多人也可能很好有奖金占亚马逊的各种津贴，等等。如果我们正在订阅一切，到处都是，为什么不将家庭维护扔进混合？
 ThumbTack，现在十多年，许多人被认为是演出经济空间中最早的搬运工之一，以专注于家庭服务。然而，没有挑战。正如我们在2019年报告的那样，最后一轮对公司来说是一个艰难的一轮，看起来它可能会在平坦的估值下进来。这是在空间中其他许多其他人的脚跟 - 图钉的竞争对手包括不仅仅是其他初创公司，而且还有像亚马逊和谷歌这样的沉重困难 - 在家庭服务中努力逃亡。
 Covid-19已被证明对ThumbTack的巨大挑战，该挑战也是去年3月下岗的250人。
 “是的，看，这是一年的过山车，”Zappacosta说，当我问他这件事时。 “在Covid的早期阶段，有很多恐惧，许多人只是把它全部关闭。人们说，'我不会在家里有人。“那些月真的很可怕 - 四月，四月 - 因为目前尚不清楚要持续多久。因此，我们制定了调整，以确保我们有点会受到时间的考验。“
  “一旦人们开始掩饰更舒服，以及所有的预防措施，你都知道，需求确实开始回来。所以在7月6日，我们看到OT恢复正常水平。然后你知道，发生了什么是我们在过去几年中在平台上做出的大投资，以便招聘招聘经验，更多的电子商务，就像真的开始支付越来越多的股息。“
 他说，2020年是ThumbTack的第三年加速增长，“鉴于Covid在中间的情况下，这是一种令人惊讶的。所以我们正在进行的势头，我们从未处于更强大的立场。“
 该公司现在正在专注于它可能最好使用技术与其平台获得鞋面，不仅与其他家庭服务提供商市场相竞争，也越来越多的提升和现任“家庭管理”提供者，如超级，上个月筹集了5000万美元。 
一个有趣的想法，即Zappacosta对我来说，这可能是后来发展阶段的一部分，涉及为客户提供“巡回”他们的家园，以便ThumbTack可以更好地了解该空间，需要更好地提高注意力 个性化其主管理到所讨论的家庭和用户。  这将使公司也在美国工作，而不是在国际上扩大。 全球房间服务和维修市场每年估计为5000亿美元，而且美国令人惊讶的是这一价值的巨大40％。
note_en:
For some context, this is nearly double the valuation Thumbtack had when it last raised money, a  $150 million round led by Sequoia in 2019. It also comes on the heels of a strong 2020, where its revenue grew more than 50%. Thumbtack’s gross revenue (what’s paid in service fees, not Thumbtack’s cut) is now on track, it says, to cross $2 billion this year.
 This latest funding is being led by the Qatar Investment Authority (QIA), with participation also from Blackstone Alternative Asset Management (BAAM) as well as G Squared. Previous backers Ballie Gifford, CapitalG, Founders Circle Capital, Sequoia Capital, and Tiger Global Management also invested.
  The funding and shifting focus of the company underscore some of the changes we have seen among consumers in the last year.
 In large part, because of Covid-19, most of us have been staying home — which has often included also moving to new homes to accommodate spending so much more time in them — and, as Thumbtack sees it, that has led many more people to paying more attention to how to make those homes better places, and to keep them in better shape overall. This is partly why it sees a big opportunity — alongside being a marketplace to find pro’s for one-off jobs you might need done around the house — in providing a “home management” platform to cater to that new attention.
 “It’s pretty wild that people’s homes their biggest assets, the most complicated thing you’re responsible for, but it doesn’t come with a manual,” Zappacosta said in an interview. “When you think about your car, you need to do regular service. Otherwise, you may have to pay a big emergency bill [to fix something]. That same concept applies to the home. But there’s never been an easy way to know what you should do, how much to pay for it, and then easily get it done. So that’s what we’re doing.”
 The services include emergency repairs of electrics in the home, plus preventative maintenance that you may need to do to on your property, he said. They don’t include contractors to refit bathrooms or other elective work.
 The move into home management is an interesting turn for another reason, too. It’s a sign of how Thumbtack is playing into a bigger change in how many consumers engage with services overall: by way of subscriptions.
 That is to say, while many of us will still go out and shop for items, or go to a cinema every now and then, we are all also subscribing to at least one video-on-demand service, many of us may well also have Prime accounts to get Amazon’s various perks, and so on. If we are subscribing for everything and everywhere else, why not throw home maintenance into the mix?
 Thumbtack, now over ten years old, is thought by many as one of the earliest movers in the gig economy space to focus on home services. That has not come without challenges, however. As we  reported back in 2019, the last round was a tough one for the company and looked like it might come in at a flat valuation. That was on the heels of many others in the space — competitors of Thumbtack’s that included not just other startups but also heavy hitters like Amazon and Google — fizzling out in their own efforts in home services.
 And Covid-19 proved to be a huge challenge for Thumbtack, too, which  laid off 250 people in March last year.
 “Yeah, look, it was a roller coaster of a year,” Zappacosta said when I asked him about that. “In the early phases of COVID, there was so much fear that many just shut it all down. People said, ‘I’m not going to have anybody in my home.’ And so those months were really scary — March, April — because it was unclear how long that was going to last. And so we made adjustments to ensure that we sort of would stand the test of time.”
  “Once people began getting more comfortable with masking up, and all the precautions, you know, demand did start to come back. And so by June, July, we saw ot get back to normal levels. And then you know, what happened is the big investments that we’ve been making in the platform in the last few years to make the hiring experience, much more e-commerce, like really began to pay more and more dividends.”
 He said that 2020 was Thumbtack’s third year of accelerating growth, “which is sort of surprising given that Covid was in the middle of that. And so the momentum that we’re carrying out, is we’ve never been in a stronger position.”
 The company now is focusing on where it might best use technology to get the upper hand with its platform, which competes not just with other home services provider marketplaces, but also a growing number of upstart and incumbent “home management” providers like Super,  which raised $50 million last month.
 One interesting idea that Zappacosta described to me, which may well be part of a later phase of development, involved providing a way for customers to “tour” their homes so that Thumbtack could get a better idea of the space and what needs attention to better personalize its home management to the home and user in question.
 That will keep the company working also in the U.S. he said, rather than expanding internationally. Globally the home services and repair market is estimated to be worth some $500 billion annually, and amazingly the U.S. represents a massive 40% of that value.
title: Home Services Platform ThumbTack在3.2亿美元的价格上提高275 000,000美元，以加倍自制管理
title_en: Home services platform Thumbtack raises $275M on a $3.2B valuation to double down on home management

亚非福顿的大多数属性包括Amazon.com和Zappos.com正在防止谷歌的跟踪系统絮状物 - 或联合学习的队列 - 根据网站代码收集反映亚马逊广阔电子商务宇宙产品研究的宝贵数据通过挖掘多达三个技术专家分析，帮助挖掘挖掘码头。
 由于Google的系统收集有关人员网络的数据，以告知其如何对其进行分类，亚马逊的下雷达举措不仅可以对谷歌的使命来指导饼干死后的数字广告跟踪未来的重大打击 - 它可以给亚马逊a腿部以自己的努力销售在开放网上剩下的左侧的广告。
 “这一举措与谷歌试图为第三方饼干提供替代方案直接相关，”数字代理Gogway集团的企业伙伴关系副总裁Amanda Martin表示。她叫亚马逊的选择阻止了大部分地点的絮状赛的另一个例子是谷歌，Apple，Facebook和亚马逊正在制作，因为数据隐私压力强迫遭到互联网上的数据跟踪基础：第三方饼干。在三位技术人员的帮助下，挖掘最后一周在亚马逊向其数字属性添加了代码，以阻止Floc使用Google Chrome浏览器跟踪访问者。例如，虽然在本周的早期wholefoods.com和woot.com未包括块絮凝的代码，但到了星期四的挖掘，那些网站确实特征代码告诉谷歌的系统不包括他们访问者的活动，以告知队列或分配ID。但亚马逊的阻止出现散落。虽然其中一位技术人员看到了阻止絮凝的那些网站，另一个没有，另一个没有，并且说亚马逊的部署可能在国家不同地区的不同服务器中明显。
 然而，在整个食物页面上有一个关于絮凝物的警告。虽然此处提到的其他亚马逊拥有的域，但是块絮凝物使用Google的推荐方法，涉及从HTML页面发送响应标题，但整个食品阻止使用策略从Amazon Analytics请求发送退出标题。一个人要求不被命名为这个故事的技术专家表示，这是值得注意的，因为亚马逊为大多数网站实施了谷歌推荐的方法，因此“已知为100％效力”，据该技术人员说用于阻止整个食品页面的絮凝物的方法可能是对亚马逊部分的监督或故意选择，可能是对某种的考验。谷歌表示，Floc旨在保护人们的隐私，因为它使用机器学习基于他们已查看的网页对它们进行分组而不是在个人级别跟踪它们。现在，系统处于导频阶段，组装数据指示人们感兴趣的网站，内容和产品。为什么亚马逊阻止絮凝物
 虽然似乎很明显，亚马逊希望在任何谷歌计划中抛出扳手，但该公司有很多原因阻碍了絮絮的成功。
 首先，亚马逊希望保护其知识产权 - 珍贵的数据显示，它显示了人们研究，审查和在线购买的产品。随着购物者的涌入预计将于6月21日和22日的主要日子涌入该网站，现在是施加电动栅栏的时间，防止谷歌喂掉该宝贵的数据槽。正如挖掘通讯上周报告的，广告科技公司和机构正在收集和分析絮凝ID数据，以提高靶向和识别能力。简而言之，它不是亚马逊的最大兴趣，让像谷歌或其他广告科技公司这样的外人利用其宝贵的购物者数据。
 如果没有亚马逊游客在混合中，谷歌的絮状物可能处于劣势，这是一个匿名的挖掘那个机构高管表示。亚马逊选择没有阻止絮状块，公司可以通过启用“明显改善某些市场购物流的结果”来协助谷歌，说。已经，谷歌的索赔对方法的表现已经受到审查。 
亚马逊的举动是谷歌购物者数据收集的“巨大限制”，这是一位在研究这个故事的研究中提供挖掘和要求不被命名的技术人员。 “你浏览亚马逊的是你是谁，”与广告技术相关的数据一起使用的来源说。
 此外，阻塞絮状物是竞争决定。亚马逊拥有自己的愿望，可以通过在亚马逊属性之外销售数字广告来抓取更多广告谷歌控件。随着亚马逊的需求侧平台业务起飞，该公司计划通过亚马逊的出版商服务部门来推出跟踪和测量广告的标识符，并通过发布商通过发布商。 “它加强了[亚马逊] DSP提出来阻止它，”代理执行。
  “为什么要给谷歌一英寸？”另外亚马逊专注的机构高管们在匿名的条件下与挖掘那些讨论过。
 但是，在阻碍絮状物成为第三方Cookie跟踪的首选替代方案时可以帮助亚马逊，而该公司通过阻止絮凝物可以对其的一些信息丢失。与防止跟踪方法的其他出版商一样，亚马逊将无法拦截线索絮凝物ID，提供窗口进入人们的行为。可以说，考虑到它处于处置的登录购物者信息的格式，信息对亚马逊的价值有限。为什么亚马逊网站在弗洛克亚马逊拥有的电子商务网站，包括全方位，Zappos，ShopBop和Goodreads显示块的絮凝剂;然而，至少在这个故事的出版时，亚马逊拥有的书籍网站abebook没有阻止它。
 第二个机构主管推测，亚马逊可以让谷歌的追踪者借助谷歌的追踪者来注意到的内容和书籍观察员的观察员。 “他们不仅仅是做的东西，”关于亚马逊的故意方法，执行官说。 “这必须是某种测试，”exec说。例如，虽然亚马逊在内部有很多关于与书籍有关的人的兴趣，但公司可以了解哪些絮凝物ID告诉它在网站外的Abebooks游客的利益。
 亚马逊可能还希望了解自己的广告跟踪和定位在某种程序的控制测试中如何对谷歌进行堆叠。 “然后你可以在没有告诉他们的情况下把谷歌放在自己的赛马中，”exec说。
 但是，亚马逊可能计划在Ad Tech研究员Krzysztof Franaszek进行Floc Research并帮助验证亚马逊的絮絮上为这个故事验证亚马逊的絮絮上，这也计划停止在Abebook上停止絮絮跟踪。公司“可能有不同的团队负责这些网站，”他说。 “也许他们没有绕过它。”
note_en:
Most of Amazon’s properties including Amazon.com, WholeFoods.com and Zappos.com are preventing Google’s tracking system FLoC — or Federated Learning of Cohorts — from gathering valuable data reflecting the products people research in Amazon’s vast e-commerce universe, according to website code analyzed by Digiday and three technology experts who helped Digiday review the code.
 As Google’s system gathers data about people’s web travels to inform how it categorizes them, Amazon’s under-the-radar move could not only be a significant blow to Google’s mission to guide the future of digital ad tracking after cookies die — it could give Amazon a leg up in its own efforts to sell advertising across what’s left of the open web.
 “This move is in direct correlation with Google’s attempt to provide an alternative to the third-party cookie,” said Amanda Martin, vp of enterprise partnerships at digital agency Goodway Group. She called Amazon’s choice to block FLoC on most of its sites another example of the chess moves Google, Apple, Facebook and Amazon are making as data privacy pressures force the destruction  of the foundation of data tracking across the internet:  the third party cookie.   With the help of three technologists, Digiday watched last week as Amazon added code to its digital properties to block FLoC from tracking visitors using Google’s Chrome browser. For example, while earlier in the week WholeFoods.com and Woot.com did not include code to block FLoC, by Thursday Digiday saw that those sites did feature code telling Google’s system not to include activities of their visitors to inform cohorts or assign IDs. But Amazon’s blocking appears scattered. While one of the technologists saw both of those sites blocking FLoC, another did not, and said Amazon’s deployment could be evident on different servers in different parts of the country.
 There is a caveat regarding FLoC blocking on Whole Foods pages, however. While other Amazon-owned domains mentioned here that block FLoC do so using Google’s recommended approach involving sending a response header from HTML pages, Whole Foods blocking employs a tactic that sends an opt-out header from Amazon analytics requests. One technologist who asked not to be named for this story said the distinction is noteworthy because the approach Amazon has implemented for most sites employs the approach recommended by Google, and therefore “known to be 100% effective,” according to the technologist, who said the approach used to block FLoC on Whole Foods pages could be an oversight or a deliberate choice on Amazon’s part, possibly as a test of some kind.   Google says FLoC is intended to protect people’s privacy because it uses machine learning to group them based on the web pages they have viewed rather than tracking them at the individual level. Right now, the system is in a pilot phase, assembling data indicating what websites, content and products people are interested in.   Why Amazon is blocking FLoC
 While it may seem obvious that Amazon would want to throw a wrench in any Google initiative, the company has plenty of reasons for hindering FLoC’s success.
 First, Amazon wants to protect its intellectual property — the prized data it has that shows what products people research, review and buy online. With an influx of shoppers expected to flood to the site on its Prime Days on June 21 and 22, now is the time to put up an electric fence preventing Google from feeding off that valuable data trough. Already, as reported by Digiday last week, ad tech firms and agencies are  gathering and analyzing FLoC ID data in the hopes of improving targeting and identification capabilities. Put simply, it is not in Amazon’s best interest to let outsiders like Google or other ad tech firms take advantage of its valuable shopper data.
 Without Amazon visitors in the mix, Google’s FLoC could be at a disadvantage, said one agency executive who spoke anonymously with Digiday. Had Amazon chosen not to block FLoC, the company could have assisted Google by enabling “significantly improved results of certain in-market shopping FLoCs,” said the exec. Already, Google’s claims about the performance of the method have been  subject to scrutiny.
 Amazon’s move is a “huge restriction” on Google’s shopper data gathering, said one of the technologists who assisted Digiday in research for this story and asked not to be named. “What you browse on Amazon is who you are,” said the source, who works with ad tech-related data.
 Plus, blocking FLoC is a competitive decision. Amazon has its own aspirations for grabbing more of the ad dollars Google controls by selling digital ads outside Amazon properties. As Amazon’s demand-side platform business takes off, the firm  plans to launch an identifier for tracking and measuring ads sold through the DSP and by publishers through Amazon’s publisher services division. “It strengthens [Amazon’s] DSP offering to block it,” said the agency exec.
  “Why give Google an inch?” asked another Amazon-focused agency executive who also spoke with Digiday on condition of anonymity.
 However, while impeding FLoC from becoming a preferred alternative to third-party cookie tracking could help Amazon, the company loses out on some information that could be of value to it by blocking FLoC. Like other  publishers preventing the tracking method, Amazon will not be able to intercept the clues FLoC IDs provide that give a window into people’s behavior. Arguably, that information is of limited value to Amazon considering the trove of logged-in shopper information it has at its disposal.   Why an Amazon site is letting in FLoC   Amazon-owned e-commerce sites including WholeFoods, Zappos, ShopBop and GoodReads display code that block FLoC; however, at least at the time of this story’s publication, Amazon-owned bookselling site AbeBooks was not blocking it.
 Amazon could have a motive for enabling Google’s trackers to take note of what content and books AbeBooks visitors look at, the second agency executive speculated. “They don’t just do stuff,” said the exec regarding Amazon’s deliberate approach. “This has got to be a test of some kind,” said the exec. For instance, while Amazon has lots of data internally about people’s interests related to books, the company could want to learn what FLoC IDs tell it about the interests of AbeBooks visitors outside the site, they added.
 Amazon might also want to see how its own ad tracking and targeting stacks up against Google’s in a control test of some sort. “You could then put Google in their own horse race without telling them,” said the exec.
 But, Amazon might be planning to stop FLoC tracking on AbeBooks, too, according to ad tech researcher Krzysztof Franaszek, who has  conducted FLoC research and helped verify Amazon’s FLoC blocking for this story. The company “probably has different teams responsible for those websites,” he said. “Maybe they haven’t gotten around to it.”
title: 分析：Amazon的属性，包括Amazon.com，Wholefoods.com和Zappos.com，阻止了Google的新絮凝物跟踪系统收集数据
title_en: Analysis: Amazon's properties, including Amazon.com, WholeFoods.com, and Zappos.com, are blocking Google's new FLoC tracking system from gathering data

  金融支持可能意味着中国球员在华为所指责的若干各国州的若干政府被推出的关键市场所指责的时候，中国球员在公司举行的一段时间。
 由于华为周围的地缘政治紧张局势，法国国有银行Caisse desdépôts - 这是一个Qwant DesDépôts - 根据参加5月18日会议的人民投票赞成金融业务。

但该机制也使得华为等投资者可以在严格的条件下将债券转变为股份。作为股东，该公司可能会获得公司的能见度或影响力＆＃39;策略。
 除其他条件外，本公司需要获得“行政授权”，然后在询问两年后的转换，并成为Qwant的股东只有“5％至7.5％”，该文件读。双方拒绝发表评论谁将发出此类授权。
  Qwant发言人表示，债券融资是与华为现有伙伴关系的延续，这并不意味着中国公司将采取股权股权。
 ＆＃34;这是通过债券的投资。它不是进入Qwant＆＃39的资本。它是一种资助我们扩展的车辆，重要的是，它与一个明确的全球参与者认识到我们的技术的稳定性以及我们项目的现实。＆＃34;

 华为没有立即回复评论请求。 Axel Springer的发言人，也是Qwant的投资者通过Axel Springer数字企业，拒绝评论。 Axel Springer是政客欧洲的共同主。
 Huawei＆＃39;与Qwant的合作伙伴日期返回到2020年3月，当两家公司宣布他们将在移动设备上合作，默认在一些欧洲国家的华为P40智能手机上安装了Qwant。由于与美国政府冲突，华为被剥夺了谷歌的产品。 
自2017年11月以来，Qwant-Huawei伙伴关系已经在管道中，有几个内部人士告诉政治家。  该交易的财务细节尚未披露，但它们包括共享广告收入，即P40用户将在Qwant的搜索引擎上生成。  本文是Politico Premium Tech Policy Roverage的一部分：Pro技术。 我们的专家新闻和政策情报工具套件允许您无缝搜索，跟踪和解塑造欧盟技术政策的发展和利益相关者，并驾驶决策影响您的行业。 通过电子邮件发送[电子邮件保护]与代码'tech'进行免费试用。
note_en:
PARIS — French search engine Qwant is seeking €8 million from Chinese telecom giant Huawei in an effort to stay afloat, according to documents seen by POLITICO.
 Qwant CEO Jean-Claude Ghinozzi asked shareholders on May 18 to accept €8 million in funding via convertible bonds from Hubble, Huawei’s venture capital arm based in Hong Kong, the documents show.
 The move comes as Qwant, which is partly funded by French public money, aims to shore up its finances. Launched in 2013 and soon backed by French President  Emmanuel Macron and European Commission Vice President  Margrethe Vestager, Qwant was designed to become a rival to U.S. search giant Google and bolster the EU's tech sector amid wider efforts to wean the Continent off dependence on foreign companies.
  Financial backing could mean the Chinese player gains a say over the company's operations, at a time when Huawei stands accused by several governments of spying and has been pushed out of key markets.
 Despite geopolitical tensions  surrounding Huawei, the French state-owned bank Caisse des Dépôts — a Qwant shareholder — voted in favor of the financial operation, according to one of the people who attended the May 18 meeting.
 The Caisse des Dépôts declined to comment. A person involved in the matter said the bank had been reassured by the fact that Huawei did not have the power to convert its bonds as it wanted.
 This type of financial operation, which is quite common in the startup world, aims to bail out Qwant after years of losses via a three-year loan at a 4.5 percent interest rate. The company lost  €13 million in 2020, € 23 million in 2019 and  €11.2 million in 2018 for revenues that amount respectively for  €7.5 million,  €5.8 million and  €3 million.
 But the mechanism also makes it possible for investors like Huawei to turn its bonds into shares under strict conditions. As a shareholder, the company could potentially gain visibility or influence on the company's strategy.
 Among other conditions, the company needs to obtain “administrative authorizations,” before asking for a conversion in two years and become a shareholder owning only “between 5 and 7.5 percent” of Qwant, the document reads. The parties declined to comment on who would issue such an authorization.
  A spokesperson for Qwant said the bond financing was a continuation of an existing partnership with Huawei, which did not mean the Chinese firm would take an equity stake.
 "This is an investment via a bond. It is not an entry into Qwant's capital. It is a vehicle to finance our expansion, and importantly it's with a crucial global player that recognizes the solidity of our technology and the reality of our project."
 "Concretely, with its bond investment, Huawei is helping Qwant to develop and gain scale on a European level for all of the brand's smartphones that will be put on the market in France, Germany and Italy," the spokesperson added.
 Huawei did not immediately respond to a request for comment. A spokesperson for Axel Springer, which is also an  investor in Qwant via Axel Springer Digital Ventures, declined to comment. Axel Springer is a co-owner of POLITICO Europe.
 Huawei's partnership with Qwant dates back to March 2020 when the two companies  announced they would cooperate on mobile devices, with Qwant being installed by default on Huawei’s P40 smartphones in some European countries. Huawei has been deprived of Google’s products because of a conflict with the American administration.
 The Qwant-Huawei partnership had been in the pipeline since November 2018, several insiders told POLITICO.
  Financial details of the deal have not been disclosed, but they included sharing advertising revenue that P40 users would generate on Qwant’s search engine.
 This article is part of  POLITICO ’s premium Tech policy coverage: Pro Technology. 让加内特R我走进我家附近的一家小型杂货店和主人，一个害羞但又善于社交的人，抬头看着我说，“你是谁，还是另一个？”
 有些单身人士说，我相信他们。成为一个双胞胎是光荣的。我们得到了很多关注。有时这是一个侮辱性的船上曾经说过，“甚至不打扰我哪个;我不记得“ - 关注注意力，感觉很好。另外，我们互相拥有，这不是一件小事。
 走出雨中的一个拥挤的前庭，她是，我在陌生人中的脸。宽慰。我们把滴下的脸颊一起压在一起并立即变成了，如果不是一个身体，那么一种需要两个运营商的傀儡，我钓鱼用她的包，她咬到我的口袋里的能量酒吧。
 楼梯，周围环绕着恐吓年轻人，我们抱着我们的地面，似乎是强迫和笑，不需要其他任何人。我想，我把我的第二次带到了这个决斗。您可能知道在兄弟姐妹中采取骄傲的避难所的感觉，知道如何组装和拆卸您的人，与您共享血液，历史，记忆的人。想象一下，不仅分享所有的头发，皮肤，虹膜，乳头，同样痛苦的痛苦在同一个颈椎圆盘中引起的，同样的笑声和笑线，同样的早期痕迹;同样的面孔 - 你的脸，证明你的你的签名 - 所以你可以看待另一个人并思考，我在那里。在那里，我差不多。
 这是一项工作活动：我的妹妹和我在同一业务;事实上，我们在不同的出版公司中对其进行了完全相同的编辑能力，并且彼此直接竞争，有时甚至在拍卖中互相竞标。在她做之前，我开始发布几年，一开始我喜欢打Mentor，提供有关代理商和办公政治和作者关怀的建议;有时她甚至会要求我阅读一些事情来验证她的意见。只要我赢得，我就可以成为一个非常慷慨的姐妹母亲，甚至 - 只要我赢得。现在，就像我觉得自己的职业出现一样，被国内蔓延和母性所追求，我姐姐似乎正在起飞，她的野心突然宣称自己，好像填充了我留下的真空。我已经停止去上班事 - 我从来没有喜欢他们，和一个小孩子，我有一个非常出色的借口，不要参加他们 - 而她表现出一切都会出现一切。当我跳过一个大型文学福利或代理混合器时，我几乎肯定会在第二天听到同事的​​话，“昨晚我挥手在整个房间里，但你没有看到我，”或“等等，昨天你有一个不同的发型。“
    它会给我带来一个奇怪的骄傲和恐慌，听到我自己的球体中的姐姐。对我们来说，会见人们一直涉及一种种植的旗帜。系统就是这样的：如果你先见我，我已经在你身上举起了旗帜;即使我们不是朋友而且永远不会是，我猜我觉得你的意思是一定的忠诚，我想我希望你能看到我比我姐姐更真实。如果她先遇到你，那么好，她已经种植了她的旗帜，我辞职为“另一个”。这些天，我发现自己感到越来越多，更像是另一个 - 不是正确的，而不是一个，而不是“那个”。这种感觉没有什么新鲜事（尽管在某种程度上，它是为了与削减地面的感觉而言，我正在发现，附带父母身份）。它是，始终是自然双循环的一部分。她正在转过身。
 我妹妹度过了美好的一年。在活动中，人们一直伸出她。 “你有一个美好的一年！”他们说，我会微笑和点头说，“她真的有。”经过一个祝贺者走开我的妹妹靠近我，低声说：“我该对他们说什么？ “谢谢，呃，你没有！”我笑了。房间很安静，因为读者迈出了舞台，我们站在一起，肩负着肩膀，手臂在腰部徘徊，自我连on。像往常一样，她的近似淹没了我，首先，对着深刻的和平和幸福感，第二，对我的比较价值的焦虑。例如，我意识到是较短的双胞胎。当这种一英寸差异在我们之间追身时，没有人可以说;在我们近三十之前，我们没有开始注意到它。也许她做了更多的瑜伽，伸长自己。也许某些章节发育了我的增长。也许我一直在萎缩。 
几分钟进入阅读，突然，没有警告，她的身体从我的身上移开，让我孤独和寒冷，好像她一直避开我，或者通过触摸我的衣服去除我的衣服。她拉开了，轻轻地走过了听众的安静房间，到了一群坐在舞台附近的人。我看着她倾斜向他们，交换温暖的微笑和呼气者问候，然后把她带回给我，折回她的长腿，然后沉没，直到她全新的理发中的一部分消失。
 在诗歌阅读中为卫生间的一行，我用一个名叫Daniel的年轻人熟悉熟悉的谈话。他表示惊讶她和我都是书籍编辑，我告诉他，相同的双胞胎拥有同样的工作，我从国家地理纪录片中学到的东西真的很常见。 “我猜这是有道理的，”丹尼尔说，然后他告诉我一对相同的双胞胎，他知道谁是谁是诗人。 “哪一个更好？”我问他，作为一个笑话。没有错过他点头的节拍，说：“这很多。”然后他给了我一个明确的答案。
 心理学家（和双胞胎）Barbara Klein在镜子里独自写道：孪生在治疗中，她2012年关于单身世界的斗争斗争。在许多方面，我的复合身份与我姐姐是家。我想留在家里，在克莱因的舒适的潮流学中，但她立即接近面孔并破坏它：“同时，双胞胎有权不同，创造自己的感觉。”作为一个青少年，我渴望运动这个权利。现在我担心，无论发生什么，我都未完成，Synecdoche，半苹果。
 你对两个分享脸部的人做了什么？旧的方式是倾向于多普普朗格的好奇心，有一些乐趣。父母可能会在配套服装中穿着双胞胎，甚至可能是挑剔的他们。到20世纪80年代，这个模型已成为可疑的（也许是冷战的症状，也许是不信任的，也许是什么？）;现在每个双胞胎都被要求成为个人。在美国，“你好吗？”和“你是特别的吗？”是同一个问题。我们都必须平等，但也不同，特别是特殊，所以双胞胎，谁是相同的，但不同，令人眼花缭乱，永远保持警惕的核算是必要的。红色工作服和一件蓝色的衬衫;蓝色工作服和另一件红色衬衫。生日和圣诞节的完全相同的礼物，但不是相同的礼物。一盒彩色铅笔为一个和一个串珠套装，与我们的一种编码相同（表观重复显示明显的内部！）。
 成年人不会比较和对比我的姐姐和我大声抱怨一些孩子和家人，但他们凝视着。有时新的熟人（通常是一个男人）将站在我们之间来回望着我们，然后说，“是的，我看到了差异。”因为我徒劳无息，我总是想问一下，“它是什么？有什么区别？”
 母性掌握最终的承诺，提升不平等。对于这个人，我的孩子，我是单数，不可替代。我没有平等。
 我从未觉得更多的单例比在我孩子出生后的最初几个月。当我姐姐带她去时，她哭了，错过了我的气味。现在她年纪大了，这是我姐姐的脖子，她抱着我试图拉开她的时候，我可以听到我的声音，因为我哄她。 
即使我经历了建立自己生活的动作，在许多方面，我的妹妹仍然拥有它的意义的关键。例如，我相信我幸福地结婚了。我的丈夫和我一起在一起多年了，如果我们曾经互相感受到的一些激情和欲望是由常规，熟悉程度和父母身份的压力消失的，那不是悲剧;它发生在很多婚姻中。但是，如果我的妹妹，其姐姐与我的姐姐相似，那么明天要出去疯狂地爱上一些新人，踏上惊心动魄，恐怖浪漫的冒险，我的婚姻的性格将被转变。向外，我的生命中没有任何改变，但我的幸福的故事将被撤消。一个总是“做得更好”。更快乐。更健康。更薄的。时间行动在我们身上。饮食。香烟。焦虑。分娩。我曾经私下则注意我的山雀是贪婪的;现在我养了这一点不再是真的。我觉得她的牙齿可能稍微白。我的鼻子是尖端的。她的嘴唇更饱满。我可以继续。
 嫉妒。弗朗西斯培根称之为“卑鄙的感情，最堕落”。苏格拉底是“灵魂的溃疡”。这是，评论家剑奈·尼吉告诉我们，一种丑陋的感觉，意味着它是非宣泄的，从“阻塞机构的情况”产生。你无能为力地阻止它，没有什么可以控制它，没有什么可以把它带到任何结论。它将与您有其可互导的方式。与兰德激情 - 愤怒，绝望 - 它持续的同时提供“不满意的美德......也没有任何治疗或净化释放。”
 毫不奇怪，尼采和Ayn Rand都将其视为浮雕输家省：“无能为力的报复”（Nietzsche），“第二次赌注的标志”（兰德）。根据Melanie Klein的说法，它是“从生命开始的手术”，“影响最早的关系”。它发生了，奥地利社会学家Helmut Schoeck，“一旦两个人能够相互比较”。在我们姐姐早期的当我的妹妹在她的鸿沟时，我总是垂死她的角度，使用巴伦镜和公共浴室和商店窗户来捕捉秘密瞥见。我记得每当我们的眼睛在反射中遇到的时候，我觉得我觉得有多变态，她抓住了我嫉妒的行为。我永远不会比我从事这个隐蔽的观看和评估，把身体视为人类镜子的人。但我仍然这样做。我窥探她。她会走路或哭泣或跳舞或穿着或试图告诉我一些重要的东西，我会意识到我的眼睛正在扫描她，好像她是一个条形码。你希望你的相同双胞胎是美丽的，确认你是美丽的，但你也希望她要丑陋，确认她比你更丑陋。
 亚里士多德把它放在这样的：“我们羡慕那些在我们靠近我们的人，地方，年龄或声誉......那些占有或成功的人是对我们的责备：这些是我们的邻居和等于;因为很明显，我们错过了我们错过了有问题的好事。“错过了这里的刀扭曲的词，这么多嫉妒与近乎小姐的感觉有关，几乎。
 在她的论文中“嫉妒和感激”，Melanie Klein将其追溯到母亲的身体上。我发现她的写作很难理解，所以，最近，当我发现自己坐在一个晚宴上旁边的精神分析师旁边时，我问他是否可以为我打破它。他说，“从婴儿的角度来看乳房：我遭受痛苦，我想要，我是一个人。然后，仿佛魔术，培养对象出现并猝灭，每次呼吸，都消除每一个焦虑，在安全和爱的茧中包裹我。“
    虽然他在说话时，服务器落后于我们，并将红葡萄酒倒入他的沉重碗中（栖息在其细长，女性茎，非常乳房，非常乳房），我在思考我如何想要一些以及我可以提出的事情因为它并采取温暖的，酸啜饮并突破四年的清醒，而不是因为我击中了任何岩石底部，而是因为我是无权渴的。
 “所以，”继续分析师，“母亲拥有最终的东西。但她并不总是放弃，至少不够快，这必须意味着她对自己保持一些。产生嫉妒。“ 
寻求摧毁的欲望：这是嫉妒的一个定义。 “专业小偷比纵向师的痛苦不那么折磨，而不是纵向，”斯文队写道。
 Klein谈到母乳喂养，在某种程度上，“与母亲失去了产前统一”。我的妹妹和我从不母乳喂养;我们太小而弱，才能吮吸得足够。但是，用双胞胎，它永远不会只是胎儿和母亲;总是有三角测量。我们不仅依赖于我们的母亲来滋养，而且互相分享而不是偷。我们每个人都扣留来自我们每个人的另一个人构成，对于另一个人来说，通过赋予全部性来结束所有不适的事情。
 在我们年轻人的卡通恶棍。邪恶的继母，非婚生子，孤独的巫师。其中，我特别用乌苏拉州识别，海巫师在小美人鱼中。随着她的水晶球间谍，她发光的眼睛（羡慕的看起来），以及她对Triton的三管金髯洞的渴望，她正在嫉妒的意大利的意大利。在一点，一双巨大的豌豆绿色的手中蒸发出来，悬挂在翼状翅膀上一秒钟，然后抚摸着朝向阿里尔的脸，就像一个让你角质的糟糕的气味。阿里尔把她的头扔回后，当手穿透她的喉咙，没有GAG反射，她的眼睛闭着眼睛，她的脖子被笼罩着。
 即使作为一个幼儿，我也知道那些手的意思，如果我不小心我自己会看到。
 是在纽约时报的十个最佳书籍列表，一个我从未实现过的职业里程碑。我发短信给她：“omggggg！恭喜!!!“我脸上尖叫着。在她的成年人和个人成功的那一刻，我用我的宝贝名字。我知道我要问她。道歉？否定？放心？或者也许我警告她，发出刺耳的警报。介意我们之间的差距。一个胜利是允许的，但要小心。
 我打电话给我的母亲，打破我姐姐的胜利的消息，以这种方式让它成为我的。这不是令人兴奋吗？我们不是这么自豪吗？我不必假装兴奋和自豪;我很兴奋，骄傲，也只是悲惨和空虚。我的母亲高兴。 “哇，”她说，“那是 - ”，她在结束前暂停了一个分裂，“你们两个真的是什么。”
 面对厨房用桌子放学后吃完同样的小吃 - 两种水果卷起来，一碗奶酪噗噗噗噗，看着对方咀嚼浮躁缓慢。目标是完成最后，当另一个没有什么没有东西。有时候我似乎先完成，她会简单地涂上她剩下的宝藏，然后，一旦她吞下了每一个面包屑，我会带出我隐藏在膝盖上的morsel，并展示了品尝它的表现。 
几年后，目标是推动静止的装饰，另一种没有完成。当，在十八岁时，她从海外几个月回来时，我们第一次持续的身体分离，她已经失去了大量的体重。我试过限制，并设法饿死了大约二十英镑，但迟早剥夺总是让位于峡谷。我最终进入了一个患者的设施，房间和大厅和草坪勉强被诱惑突破。
 含有肥沃的厌食症的Willpower;厌食症希望他们可以像我们那样松散。 （很难想到一个更具破坏性的“治疗方法”，它比在一个屋檐下带来一堆吃着无序的女孩和妇女，以便他们可以继续狂热的比较狂欢，但这就是这个设施所做的，我们的父母为他们支付了它现在令我兴奋地击败了我，其中包括狂欢，令人羡慕的丑陋，通过饥饿，或者释放一次，或者释放一次。
 我的室友是一个五十岁的郊区家庭主妇，在她的存在中暂定，她的眼球似乎颤抖着他们的插座。在家里，她每天吃一杯葡萄;在我们不得不咀嚼的设施，像我们其他人一样咀嚼并吞下她的饭。
 一只瓦伦肮脏的金发女郎被她的地下室跑步机驱动了疯狂。我从来没有听过她完整的句子，这些判决没有参考这个跑步机：她所做的数千英里，燃烧了多少小时和卡路里，哪个人说她忍受留下了深刻的印象，这种坏家庭成员试图让她留下深刻的印象她的机器。她花了四分之一的生命，冲刺到位，她会以同样的方式耗尽剩下的。
 我现在想起这些女性，以及在每次会议上有多明显，我们都是（患者和辅导员）在精神上比较了房间里的尸体和附着在他们身上的故事。我想知道为什么我们这么多关于食物，体重，家庭，性别，但绝不是一个融合美国的人。
 它让我们羞愧，inarticulate。作为Schoeck观察到的，“这是卓越的不同语言的白话形式允许一个人直接对另一个人说：'不这样做。它会让我羡慕！“”
 一个令人惊叹的美丽着名的女人张贴了自己的照片，总是带着Jokey或赋权的标题，好像骄傲的她的美丽展示代表压迫克服。每一个人都经常是一万千年之一的追随者将回复“呃，如此华丽”或“我甚至不能”或偶尔是一个友好的“我恨你”。这是我们最接近的我们通过评论饲料释放勉强包含的愤怒课程。 “天使！”人们喊道。 “纯粹的战士女神！” “yass！” “你是如此完美！”这就像我们用恭维地扔了她。 
在爆发期间，帮助育儿。今晚在厨房里，我的丈夫和我开始再次战斗一些愚蠢的事情 - 是否可以安全地在罐中制冷番茄酱。当我们的声音变得敏锐时，我的妹妹悄悄地带领我的女儿楼上，开始跑浴室。我听到了她的甜蜜，清晰的声音唱出一个关于擦洗的简易歌曲，通过Gleeful的尖叫声标题为更多。她是那种让她的整个灵魂扔进每一个睡前的姨妈。我很感激;在她逗留期间，我已经依靠她作为一个合作父母。然而，有时候我在世界上最需要的两个人之间的爱威胁我。 “你不能从孩子那里需要任何东西，”当我泪水打电话时，我的母亲说。
 在我女儿睡着后，我轻轻地在姐姐的门上敲门。她在床上坐在床上，她的笔记本电脑在她的腿上。 “Whatcha读书吗？”现在永远不是我们的无辜问题;我们经常竞争完成相同批次的提交。独自一人在我的桌子上，撇去页面，我想知道，她想到了这份手稿是什么？如果她重视它，那么我会重视它。如果她解雇它，那么我很无聊。
 我生命中的伟大梦想是与我姐姐合作成功，与她协调，与她一起闪耀，但现实是我们几乎从不管理这一点。相反，我常常被她的口才所致，被她的和平所令人不安，被她的光灭绝。她似乎取消了我。当她在她的脸上获得重量或获得疙瘩或者在某些东西上失败时，在恢复自己之前，我向内向内快乐一毫秒，然后用羞耻挤出这种可怕的快乐。
 Composer Salieri称自己是平庸的“赞助人的圣徒”，被莫扎特羡慕地折磨。在中学，我在Salieri上致电，从电影中录制了他的独白，伴随着音乐运动，并在学校的时候在我的随身携带时听他们。
